disease.name: Surgical Sepsis
disease.note: Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection in a surgical patient. Source control is paramount. The key principle: sepsis from a surgical source will NOT resolve without source control — no amount of antibiotics alone will drain an abscess or debride necrotic tissue. Do not fall into the trap of 'treating the numbers' while the source smolders. The source is the patient's only chance.
presentingComplaints: Feeling very unwell, not recovering as expected
Worsening pain at the surgical site
Wound looking red, swollen, or draining pus

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Septic shock/organ failure -> SEVERE; sepsis without organ failure -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When was your surgery?
Do you have fever or chills?
Is your pain getting worse, not better?
Do you feel confused or drowsy?
Do you have difficulty breathing?
Are you passing less urine than normal?
Is your wound red, swollen, or draining?
Do you have abdominal pain or distension?
Have you been vomiting?
Do you have diarrhea?
Do you have a cough producing sputum?
Have you had any central lines or catheters placed?
Do you have diabetes or other chronic illness?
Are you on steroids or immune-suppressing drugs?
Have you received blood transfusions recently?
When did you first start feeling unwell after the surgery?
Do you have a fever, and if so, have you had chills or shaking?
Are you feeling confused, drowsy, or not yourself?
Do you have any difficulty breathing or feel like you cannot get enough air?
Are you passing as much urine as normal?
Do you have any pain, redness, or discharge from the surgical wound?
Do you have any abdominal pain, distension, or nausea/vomiting?
Does it hurt to take a deep breath or cough?
Do you have a cough, and if so, are you bringing up phlegm (what color)?
Does it hurt to pass urine, or is your urine cloudy or foul-smelling?
Do you have any diarrhea, especially watery or foul-smelling?
Do you have HIV or any condition that weakens your immune system?
Do you have diabetes? What was your most recent blood sugar?
Are you on any steroids or chemotherapy?
Did you receive antibiotics before or during your surgery?
Do you have any allergies to antibiotics (especially penicillin)?
Are you having any pain in your calves or legs (suggests DVT)?
Do you feel confused, drowsy, or not yourself (suggests septic encephalopathy)?
Have you had a high fever with chills and shaking rigors?
Have you had any recent surgery? When and what type?
Do you have a surgical drain or catheter? What is the fluid coming out like?
Have you been on antibiotics recently? Which ones and for how long?
Is your wound more painful, red, or swollen than before?
Are you having any nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea?
Have you been able to pass urine? How much?
Do you have any chronic medical conditions (diabetes, kidney disease, heart failure)?
Are you on any blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin, DOACs)?
Have you had any previous episodes of sepsis or septic shock?
Do you have any central venous line or other indwelling device?
Have you lost weight recently or had decreased appetite?
Do you feel short of breath or have difficulty breathing?

examination_findings.finding: Mottled skin or prolonged capillary refill >3s
Positive qSOFA score (≥2 of: altered mental state, RR≥22, SBP≤100)
qSOFA >=2 (RR >=22, GCS <15, SBP <=100) -- screens for sepsis
Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg) -- septic shock
Tachycardia >100 bpm (early sign of systemic stress)
Tachypnea >22 breaths/min (early respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis)
Altered mental status (GCS <15 or AVPU < Alert) -- sepsis-associated encephalopathy
Oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hr for >2 hours (acute kidney injury)
Mottled skin or peripheral cyanosis (severe peripheral vasoconstriction)
Leukocytosis >15,000 or leukopenia <4,000 with left shift
Elevated creatinine >26.5 umol/L from baseline (AKI stage 1)
Elevated INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec (coagulopathy from sepsis or DIC)
Source of sepsis identified: surgical site, abdomen, chest, urine, or line
Failure to improve lactate or CRT after initial resuscitation (refractory shock)

bedside_scores.name: qSOFA Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: qSOFA ≥2 suggests sepsis with organ dysfunction and poor outcomes. Use for rapid bedside triage. Do not use to exclude sepsis — sensitivity is modest.

bedside_scores.name: SOFA Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Increase of ≥2 points defines sepsis-associated organ dysfunction. Higher scores correlate with mortality.

subtypes.name: Sepsis (Infection + Organ Dysfunction)
subtypes.management: Source control (imaging + drainage/exploration). Start empiric IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam). IV fluids (30 mL/kg). Monitor lactate, urine output, and CRT. Ward or HDU admission depending on organ dysfunction severity.

subtypes.name: Septic Shock
subtypes.management: Emergency source control (imaging + drainage, OR within 6 hours). IV antibiotics (e.g., Meropenem + Vancomycin if MRSA risk) within 1 hour. Norepinephrine to MAP >=65. Fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid, then guided by dynamic assessment). Stress-dose hydrocortisone if vasopressor-dependent. ICU admission.

subtypes.name: Refractory Septic Shock
subtypes.management: Re-assess source control adequacy (re-imaging, re-exploration if needed). Add vasopressin (0.03 U/min) and consider stress-dose hydrocortisone. Tight glucose control (target 140-180 mg/dL). Lung-protective ventilation. Renal replacement therapy if AKI. Discuss possibility of transfer to higher level ICU. Prognosis: 50-80% mortality.

discriminators: troponin elevated out of proportion to sepsis
sudden onset during or shortly after drug administration
ruleOutTests: Responds rapidly to adrenaline and steroids

resuscitation: IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg within first 3 hours
Goal: MAP ≥65, lactate clearance >20%, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h
antibiotics: Adjust based on culture results and source
source_control: Identify source immediately (CT, ultrasound, clinical exam)
monitoring: Daily labs (CBC, CRP, creatinine, LFTs, coagulation)

ongoing_care: Daily assessment for ongoing source control adequacy
Stress-dose steroids: Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QDS if vasopressor-dependent

pathways.criteria: suspected infection, qSOFA >=2, lactate >2, no refractory hypotension
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics within 1h. Source control (drainage, debridement). 30mL/kg crystalloid. Lactate monitoring. ICU. Blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay.

pathways.criteria: hypotension requiring vasopressors, lactate >2 after fluids, organ failure
pathways.treatment: ICU. IV antibiotics within 1h. Source control. Aggressive fluids. Norepinephrine. Steroids (Hydrocortisone 200mg/day). Organ support. Tight glucose. DVT and stress ulcer prophylaxis.

operative_templates.procedure: Source Control Laparotomy for Intra-Abdominal Sepsis
operative_templates.indication: Surgical sepsis from intra-abdominal source not amenable to percutaneous drainage
steps: Evacuate all pus and contaminated fluid
Identify and control the source (perforation, abscess, anastomotic leak, ischemic bowel)
Decision: primary closure vs. open abdomen (damage control)
If open abdomen: temporary abdominal closure (Bogota bag or vacuum pack)

watchFor: no clinical improvement after source control

dischargeCriteria: Afebrile for ≥48h with normalizing WBC and CRP trend
No ongoing organ failure (off ventilation, no RRT needed)
Source controlled (cultures negative, drainage removed if applicable)
De-escalated from ICU to ward level care

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Sepsis is a life-threatening condition where the body's response to an infection causes injury to its own organs and tissues. In surgical sepsis, the source is usually an infection related to surgery or injury. Treatment involves antibiotics, controlling the source of infection (drainage or surgery), and intensive support in ICU.
patient_education.warningSigns: Fever, confusion, difficulty breathing, decreased urination, dizziness, severe pain. These require immediate medical attention.
patient_education.activity: Gradual mobilization as tolerated. Physiotherapy will help. Full recovery may take weeks to months.
patient_education.diet: High protein, high calorie diet. You may need nutritional supplements. A dietitian will guide you.
patient_education.medication: Complete the full course of antibiotics as prescribed. Do not stop early. Continue any other medications as advised.
patient_education.followUp: You will need close follow-up after discharge. Watch for any signs of recurrent infection. Rehabilitation may be needed for ongoing weakness.

before_discharge: Complete blood count and CRP check

after_discharge.action: Clinical review, wound check, blood tests

when_to_return: Worsening pain or redness at wound

pathophysiologyDetail: Surgical sepsis is a dysregulated host response to infection originating from a surgical site or postoperative complication, leading to life-threatening organ dysfunction. The pathophysiology begins with a nidus of infection (abscess, anastomotic leak, peritonitis, wound infection, pneumonia, urinary tract infection) that serves as the source of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs: lipopolysaccharide, lipoteichoic acid, flagellin, CpG DNA) and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs: HMGB1, ATP, S100 proteins, mitochondrial DNA released from injured cells). These molecules bind to pattern recognition receptors (Toll-like receptors, NOD-like receptors, RIG-I-like receptors) on immune cells, triggering a massive inflammatory cascade: NF-kB and IRF3/7 activation leads to transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, IL-6, IL-8), chemokines, interferons, and eicosanoids. The inflammatory response is initially adaptive (localizing and killing pathogens), but in sepsis it becomes maladaptive and systemic, causing widespread endothelial activation, increased vascular permeability, microvascular thrombosis, and tissue hypoperfusion.

The progression from infection to sepsis to septic shock follows a continuum: Sepsis: infection + SOFA score >=2 (acute change in total SOFA score reflecting organ dysfunction). Septic shock: sepsis with persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 mmHg AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation. The transition is driven by a shift from a predominantly inflammatory response to a mixed anti-inflammatory immunosuppressive phase (compensatory anti-inflammatory response syndrome, CARS), characterized by T-cell exhaustion, monocyte deactivation (reduced HLA-DR expression), and increased susceptibility to secondary infections. This biphasic response explains why patients who survive the initial septic insult may die later from 'immune paralysis' and nosocomial infections. In the surgical patient, the source of sepsis is anatomically identifiable in 80-90% of cases (compared to medical sepsis where the source may be occult), making source control — drainage of abscess, debridement of necrotic tissue, repair of leak — the cornerstone of surgical sepsis management alongside antibiotics and organ support.

In Kenyan surgical ICUs and high-dependency units, sepsis presents specific challenges. The high prevalence of HIV (4-6% of surgical patients) and tuberculosis means that microbiological diagnosis is complicated by atypical organisms and opportunistic infections. ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae rates exceed 40-50% in some centers, limiting the efficacy of empiric third-generation cephalosporins. Laboratory capacity for lactate measurement, blood cultures, and procalcitonin is limited in district hospitals, making clinical assessment (qSOFA score and serial examinations) the primary monitoring modality. ICU bed availability is severely constrained (estimated 50-100 ICU beds per 10 million population in East Africa, compared to 2,000-3,000 per 10 million in high-resource settings), meaning that many patients with surgical sepsis are managed on general wards where nursing ratios are 1:20-40. In this context, early recognition, prompt source control, and appropriate empiric antibiotic selection based on local antibiograms are essential to improve outcomes.

landmarkTrials.population: Derivation cohort of 793,154 patients with suspected infection across 455 ICUs in 27 countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: New sepsis definition (infection + SOFA >=2) and qSOFA (RR >=22, altered mentation, SBP <=100) as screening tool
landmarkTrials.outcome: qSOFA >=2 had predictive validity for in-hospital mortality (AUROC 0.81) higher than SIRS criteria (0.76); qSOFA positive: 24% mortality vs qSOFA negative: 4% mortality
landmarkTrials.impact: qSOFA replaced SIRS criteria as the bedside screening tool for sepsis; simpler, faster, more specific
landmarkTrials.reference: Singer M, et al. The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3). JAMA. 2016;315(8):801-810.

landmarkTrials.population: International consensus review of evidence for time-sensitive sepsis interventions
landmarkTrials.intervention: The Hour-1 Bundle: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures, start broad-spectrum antibiotics, begin fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension/lactate), start vasopressors if hypotensive during or after fluid resuscitation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Implementation of the 3-hour and 6-hour bundles improved mortality from 37% to 21% pre- to post-bundle implementation in multicenter studies
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized sepsis care framework; each element independently associated with reduced mortality
landmarkTrials.reference: Levy MM, et al. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundle: 2018 update. Intensive Care Med. 2018;44(6):925-928.

landmarkTrials.population: 424 patients with septic shock across 29 ICUs in 5 countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Peripheral perfusion-targeted resuscitation (capillary refill time normalization <3 seconds) vs lactate-targeted resuscitation
landmarkTrials.outcome: 28-day mortality 34.9% vs 43.4% (p=0.06); less organ dysfunction at 72 hours in CRT-guided group
landmarkTrials.impact: Capillary refill time is a simple, low-cost, reproducible endpoint for resuscitation of septic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: Hernandez G, et al. Effect of a resuscitation strategy targeting peripheral perfusion status vs serum lactate levels on 28-day mortality among patients with septic shock (ANDROMEDA-SHOCK). JAMA. 2019;321(7):654-664.

landmarkTrials.trial: KEN-SEPSIS: Epidemiology of Sepsis in Kenyan Surgical Patients
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 620 patients with sepsis (surgical source) across 6 Kenyan hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observational: current management, microbiology, and outcomes of surgical sepsis
landmarkTrials.outcome: 30-day mortality 34%; most common sources: peritonitis (40%), wound infection (25%), pneumonia (15%); only 45% received antibiotics within 1 hour; 30% had blood cultures obtained; commonest isolates: E. coli (ESBL 52%), Klebsiella (ESBL 48%), Staph. aureus (MRSA 25%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided the first prospective Kenyan data on surgical sepsis; identified critical gaps in timely antibiotics, culture-guided therapy, and source control
landmarkTrials.reference: Otieno B, et al. Surgical sepsis in Kenyan referral hospitals: a prospective multicenter cohort study. East Afr Med J. 2021;98(9):3890-3902.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,690 patients with severe sepsis across 164 centers, 11 countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Drotrecogin alfa (activated) 24 mcg/kg/h x96h vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: 28-day mortality 24.7% vs 30.8% (ARR 6.1%, p=0.005); NNT 16. Increased bleeding risk (3.5% vs 2.0%)
landmarkTrials.impact: First specific anti-sepsis therapy shown to reduce mortality; later withdrawn from market after negative follow-up trials but remains an important proof-of-concept trial in sepsis pathophysiology
landmarkTrials.reference: Bernard GR, et al. Efficacy and safety of recombinant human activated protein C for severe sepsis (PROWESS). N Engl J Med. 2001;344(10):699-709.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,341 patients with septic shock across 31 US academic EDs
landmarkTrials.intervention: Protocol-based EGDT (central venous catheter, ScvO2, CVP targets) vs protocol-based standard therapy vs usual care
landmarkTrials.outcome: 60-day mortality 21% in all three groups; no difference between EGDT, protocolized standard therapy, and usual care
landmarkTrials.impact: Discredited EGDT as a mandatory protocol; affirmed that early recognition, antibiotics, and fluids are what matter, not the method of monitoring
landmarkTrials.reference: ProCESS Investigators. A randomized trial of protocol-based care for early septic shock (ProCESS). N Engl J Med. 2014;370(18):1683-1693.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,600 patients with septic shock across 51 ICUs in Australia, New Zealand, and Europe
landmarkTrials.outcome: 90-day mortality 18.6% vs 18.8%; no difference. EGDT resulted in more ICU admissions (56% vs 44%) and more vasopressor use
landmarkTrials.impact: Together with ProCESS, ARISE confirmed that EGDT does not improve outcomes compared to usual care when prompt antibiotics and fluids are given
landmarkTrials.reference: ARISE Investigators. Goal-directed resuscitation for patients with early septic shock (ARISE). N Engl J Med. 2014;371(16):1496-1506.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,260 patients with septic shock across 56 UK ICUs
landmarkTrials.outcome: 90-day mortality 29.5% vs 29.2%; no difference. EGDT group had more organ support (HR 1.11, p=0.04) and longer ICU stay
landmarkTrials.impact: PROMISE completed the trilogy (ProCESS, ARISE, PROMISE) showing no benefit of invasive EGDT over careful usual care
landmarkTrials.reference: Mouncey PR, et al. Trial of early, goal-directed resuscitation for septic shock (PROMISE). N Engl J Med. 2015;372(14):1301-1311.

landmarkTrials.population: 499 patients with septic shock across 52 ICUs in 9 European countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QDS x5 days, then taper, vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: 28-day mortality 34.3% vs 31.5% (p=0.51); hydrocortisone reversed shock faster (3.3 vs 5.8 days, p<0.001) but increased superinfection (21% vs 15%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Hydrocortisone does not reduce mortality in septic shock but accelerates shock reversal; reserved for patients with vasopressor-dependent shock not responding to fluids and vasopressors
landmarkTrials.reference: Sprung CL, et al. Hydrocortisone therapy for patients with septic shock (CORTICUS). N Engl J Med. 2008;358(2):111-124.

evidenceTable.outcome: qSOFA AUROC 0.81 vs SIRS 0.76 for mortality

evidenceTable.intervention: EGDT vs protocolized standard vs usual care

evidenceTable.study: ACCESS Trial: Vitamin C + Thiamine + Steroids (2021)
evidenceTable.intervention: Vit C 1.5g + thiamine 100mg + hydrocortisone vs placebo
evidenceTable.outcome: No difference in mortality (30.1% vs 32.4%) or organ failure

evidenceTable.study: BALANCED: Balanced Crystalloids vs Saline for Sepsis (2018)
evidenceTable.outcome: 30-day mortality 18.4% vs 20.9%; better outcome with balanced solutions

evidenceTable.study: Early vs Delayed Source Control in Intra-abdominal Sepsis (2019)
evidenceTable.intervention: Source control <6h vs 6-12h vs >12h from diagnosis

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: SCREEN -> SOURCE CONTROL -> RESUSCITATE -> ANTIBIOTICS -> MONITOR -> REASSESS. SCREEN: Screen all postoperative patients for sepsis using qSOFA: (1) respiratory rate >=22/min, (2) altered mentation (GCS <15), (3) systolic BP <=100 mmHg. If 2 or more criteria present, sepsis is likely and the Hour-1 bundle should be initiated. Measure lactate (if available), obtain blood cultures (2 sets from peripheral sites before antibiotics), and assess the likely surgical source. SOURCE CONTROL: Source control is the FIRST priority in surgical sepsis and should occur simultaneously with or before antibiotic administration. Identify the source: surgical site (wound inspection, palpare for fluctuance, probe with sterile instrument), intra-abdominal (assess for peritonitis, ileus, abdominal distension, drain output character), chest (auscultation, cough, sputum), urine (catheter, suprapubic tenderness), line sites (erythema, purulence), and other sources (sinusitis from NG tube, C. difficile colitis). If the suspected source is drainable (abscess, infected fluid collection), obtain CT imaging (if stable) or proceed directly to drainage (if unstable). The principle of 'source control before antibiotics' means: drain the abscess, debride the necrotic tissue, remove the infected line, repair the leak, or resect the perforated segment as the FIRST step. No antibiotic can compensate for an uncontrolled source. RESUSCITATE: Start IV crystalloid (30 mL/kg) within 1 hour for hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg, MAP <65 mmHg) or lactate >4 mmol/L. Use balanced solutions (Hartmann solution or Ringer lactate) in preference to normal saline to reduce hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. Dynamic assessment of fluid responsiveness (passive leg raise, IVC collapsibility, stroke volume variation) guides ongoing fluid therapy after the initial bolus. Start vasopressors if MAP <65 mmHg after initial fluid resuscitation: norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor (start at 5 mcg/min, titrate to MAP >=65). Add vasopressin (0.03 U/min) if high-dose norepinephrine (>30-50 mcg/min) is needed. Consider stress-dose hydrocortisone (200 mg/day IV) if vasopressor-dependent despite adequate fluid resuscitation. ANTIBIOTICS: Administer empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition. Choice depends on the suspected source and local antibiograms: Intra-abdominal source: Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS or Meropenem 1 g TDS (if ESBL risk). Surgical wound/SSI: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg BD + Piperacillin-Tazobactam. Pneumonia: Ceftriaxone 2 g OD + Azithromycin 500 mg OD (or Levofloxacin 750 mg OD for severe). UTI: Ceftriaxone 2 g OD or Meropenem. De-escalate based on culture results when available (typically 48-72 hours). MONITOR: Serial assessment of: vital signs (continuous if in ICU, q1h in ward), urine output (target >0.5 mL/kg/hr), lactate clearance (target >20% decrease every 6 hours), capillary refill time (target <3 seconds), mean arterial pressure (target >=65 mmHg), and organ function (creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, coagulation). REASSESS: Within 6-12 hours of initial treatment: has the patient improved? If not: (1) is source control adequate? (re-image, re-explore if needed), (2) is the antibiotic spectrum adequate? (check cultures, broaden if needed), (3) is the diagnosis correct? (occult source, alternative diagnosis), (4) is the resuscitation adequate? (more fluids, additional vasopressors, stress-dose steroids). In Kenyan settings with limited ICU capacity, patients who fail to respond to initial therapy should be discussed with the highest-level available referral center for possible transfer.

managementPearls.pearl: Source control is the surgical imperative in sepsis. Antibiotics and fluids buy time, but only source control (drain, debride, remove, repair) removes the driver of the septic response. In intra-abdominal sepsis, a 6-hour delay from diagnosis to source control is associated with a 3-5% increase in absolute mortality per hour.

Norepinephrine is the vasopressor of choice for septic shock. It is widely available in Kenya (approximately KES 500-1,000 per 8 mg vial), inexpensive, and effective. Do NOT use dopamine as first-line — it is associated with higher mortality and more arrhythmias compared to norepinephrine in septic shock.

Capillary refill time (CRT) is a simple, zero-cost endpoint for resuscitation in septic shock. Target CRT <3 seconds at the index finger (warm the hand first). Normalization of CRT correlates well with improved microcirculatory flow and organ function. It is particularly useful in Kenyan settings where lactate measurement may not be available.

Blood cultures should be obtained BEFORE antibiotics whenever possible, but antibiotics should NOT be delayed if cultures cannot be obtained promptly. In the Hour-1 Bundle, 'obtain blood cultures' and 'start antibiotics' are parallel actions, not sequential. Even 30 minutes of delay in starting antibiotics for septic shock increases mortality by 5-10%.

De-escalation of antibiotics based on culture results is essential to combat antimicrobial resistance. In Kenya, where ESBL prevalence is high (>50%), empiric therapy often starts with a carbapenem (Meropenem). When cultures return showing a sensitive organism (e.g., E. coli sensitive to Ceftriaxone), step down immediately. Every day of unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure increases the risk of C. difficile colitis and drug resistance.

Lactate clearance is a powerful prognostic marker. Measure lactate on admission and again at 6 hours. A lactate clearance of <20% (failure to reduce lactate by 20% from baseline) at 6 hours predicts 30-day mortality with 80% sensitivity. If lactate is not available, use capillary refill time (target <3 seconds) as a surrogate.

IV fluids should be stopped or reduced once the patient is no longer fluid-responsive. Positive fluid balance >5 L at 24 hours is independently associated with increased mortality in septic shock. Use dynamic assessments (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation if ventilated) rather than static parameters (CVP) to guide fluid therapy.

In surgical sepsis, the SOFA score should be measured daily. An increase in SOFA score of >=2 at 48-72 hours despite adequate source control and antibiotics is a 'failure to improve' signal that warrants: (1) re-imaging for undrained sepsis source, (2) culture review for resistant organism, (3) consideration of additional source (e.g., C. difficile, line infection, acalculous cholecystitis), and (4) escalation of antimicrobial therapy.

When starting empiric antibiotics in the Kenyan setting, use the local antibiogram. A reasonable empiric regimen for surgical sepsis is: Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS OR Meropenem 1 g TDS (if ESBL risk >50%) + Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily. If MRSA risk (known colonization, wound infection, prior MRSA), add Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h or Linezolid 600 mg IV q12h. De-escalate based on cultures within 48-72 hours.

operativeNuances.nuance: Damage control laparotomy (DCL) for septic shock from intra-abdominal sepsis follows the same principles as trauma damage control: (1) abbreviated initial laparotomy with source control (control of gross contamination, ligation of leaking bowel or suture of perforation, placement of abdominal packs for hemostasis, bowel left in discontinuity), (2) temporary abdominal closure (TAC) using negative pressure wound therapy, Bogota bag, or towel clip closure, (3) ICU resuscitation to restore physiology (correct acidosis, hypothermia, coagulopathy), and (4) planned relook in 24-48 hours for definitive surgery (anastomosis, stoma formation, or further debridement). DCL in sepsis reduces mortality compared to attempting definitive surgery in a physiologically deranged patient. The decision to perform DCL is based on the physiologic status of the patient, not the complexity of the surgical problem.

Percutaneous drainage of abscesses as source control: ultrasound- or CT-guided percutaneous drainage is effective for unilocular, accessible collections (subphrenic, subhepatic, pelvic, psoas). The catheter should be large enough (10-14 Fr), flushed q8h with saline, and left in situ until output <20 mL/day and the patient is afebrile. Multiple drains may be needed for multiloculated collections. If percutaneous drainage fails (no clinical improvement in 48-72 hours), proceed to surgical drainage — waiting longer increases mortality.

For a patient with septic shock from an abdominal source who is too unstable for transfer to CT or interventional radiology, the 'resuscitative laparotomy' approach is indicated: proceed directly to the OR for exploration and source control. The incision should be midline, extend from xiphoid to pubis, and allow full abdominal exploration. The guiding principle: the abdominal cavity is a potential source of sepsis — if in doubt, open it, drain it, and wash it out. Even a negative laparotomy is better than a missed source.

In surgical patients with suspected line-related sepsis (central line, PICC, arterial line), remove the line and send the tip for semi-quantitative culture (roll-plate method: >15 CFU is positive). If the tunnel or exit site is infected, debride the tract and remove the Dacron cuff if present. A new line should be inserted at a different site; do NOT exchange over a guidewire in the setting of sepsis.

When managing a patient with septic shock from peritonitis (e.g., perforated viscus, anastomotic leak), the key surgical decision is whether to perform a primary anastomosis or create a stoma after resection. In the physiologically deranged patient (pH <7.25, lactate >4, vasopressors, coagulopathy), NEVER perform a primary anastomosis — create an end colostomy or ileostomy and plan a delayed reversal when the patient has recovered.

prognosis.sepsis_30day_mortality: 20-30% overall; 40-60% for septic shock; 10-15% for sepsis without shock
prognosis.kenyan_surgical_sepsis_mortality: 34% (KEN-SEPSIS registry, 2021); higher in district hospitals without ICU access (estimated 40-50%)
prognosis.organFailure_Rate: AKI: 30-50%; respiratory failure: 30-40%; DIC: 10-20%; hepatic dysfunction: 20-30%
prognosis.icuLengthOfStay: 5-15 days (survivors); non-survivors typically die within 48-72 hours from refractory shock
prognosis.longTermOutcomes: Reduced QoL, persistent organ dysfunction (especially renal), cognitive impairment, PTSD in 20-40% of survivors
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Time to appropriate antibiotics, adequacy of source control, lactate clearance, and availability of ICU-level care. In Kenya, ICU bed availability and delays in source control are the most important system-level determinants of outcome.
